paper_evaluations.title: Safety and effectiveness of surgical fixation versus non-surgical methods for the treatment of flail chest in adult populations: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

item_1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as both a systematic review and meta-analysis.

item_2.reason: Objectives clearly state participants (adults with flail chest), intervention (surgical fixation), comparison (non-surgical), and outcomes (safety and efficacy).

item_3.reason: Eligibility criteria specified: RCTs comparing surgical versus non-surgical management in adults with flail chest, with defined outcome parameters.

item_4.reason: Search date provided but specific databases or information sources not named in the abstract.

item_5.reason: Risk of bias assessment mentioned but specific methods or tools used not described.

item_6.reason: Heterogeneity statistics provided but principal summary measures (e.g., risk ratio, mean difference) and synthesis methods not explicitly stated.

item_7.reason: Total number of included studies (four) and participants (157 total: 79 surgical, 78 non-surgical) clearly reported.

item_8.reason: Results reported with p-values and heterogeneity measures but actual effect sizes and confidence intervals not provided.

item_9.reason: Call for further research implied but specific limitations of the evidence not explicitly summarized.

item_10.reason: General interpretation provided: surgical intervention reduces tracheostomy, ICU time, and possibly pneumonia risk in flail chest patients.

item_11.reason: Funding sources not described in the abstract.

item_12.reason: Protocol registration number not provided in the abstract.

paper_evaluations.title: Hydrocortisone for Preventing Adverse Drug Reactions to Snake Antivenom: A Meta-Analysis.

item_1.reason: The title explicitly identifies the report as 'A Meta-Analysis.'

item_2.reason: The objective is explicitly stated: 'to evaluate the effect of prehydrocortisone on the risk of ADRs.'

item_3.reason: While PICO elements are described, explicit and comprehensive inclusion/exclusion criteria are not formally stated.

item_4.reason: Three databases named (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane) with specific search date (December 6, 2020) provided.

item_5.reason: Methods for assessing risk of bias in included studies are not described.

item_6.reason: Random-effects model stated, and odds ratio specified as the primary measure of effect.

item_7.reason: Four studies with 1348 participants reported; eight comparisons and study characteristics mentioned.

item_8.reason: Results presented with pooled OR 0.47 (95% CI 0.19-1.17), including subgroup analyses.

item_9.reason: Limitations of evidence explicitly mentioned: heterogeneity (inconsistency), quality (bias), and paucity of data (imprecision).

item_10.reason: Interpretation provided: prehydrocortisone alone ineffective, combinations with antihistamines/adrenaline may reduce ADRs.

item_11.reason: No funding source is mentioned anywhere in the abstract.

item_12.reason: No registration number or registry name is provided in the abstract.

paper_evaluations.title: Meta-analysis of the clinical efficacy of laparoscopic appendectomy in the treatment of acute appendicitis.

item_1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as a 'Meta-analysis of the clinical efficacy of laparoscopic appendectomy.'

item_2.reason: Objective clearly stated: compare postoperative recovery after LA versus OA to determine optimal treatment plan for appendectomy.

item_3.reason: Only publication dates (2010-2021) and topic mentioned; detailed inclusion/exclusion criteria (study design, patient characteristics, outcomes) not specified.

item_4.reason: Five databases explicitly named (PubMed, Web of Science, Embase, CNKI, Wanfang) with date range 2010-2021.

item_5.reason: No mention of methods used to assess risk of bias or quality appraisal of included studies.

item_6.reason: Only states 'Stata16.0 was used for meta-analysis' without describing statistical methods, effect models, or heterogeneity assessment.

item_7.reason: Reports 16 included studies with total participants: 1251 in LA group and 898 in OA group.

item_8.reason: Multiple outcomes presented with effect sizes (OR, SMD), 95% confidence intervals, and P-values for main comparisons.

item_9.reason: No discussion of certainty or strength of evidence (e.g., GRADE assessment) for the findings.

item_10.reason: Abstract does not discuss any limitations of the evidence, included studies, or review methodology.

item_11.reason: Conclusion interprets results and provides clinical implications: LA's advantages demonstrate its safety and feasibility for treating AA.

item_12.reason: Neither funding source nor systematic review registration number is mentioned in the abstract.

paper_evaluations.title: Effectiveness of Mechanical Chest Compression Devices over Manual Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation: A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis and Trial Sequential Analysis.

item_1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis and Trial Sequential Analysis.'

item_2.reason: Objective clearly stated: comparing mechanical compression devices versus manual compression for achieving ROSC after OHCA.

item_3.reason: Eligibility criteria specified: RCTs and observational studies, 2000-2020, English language, adult patients, non-trauma OHCA, ROSC outcomes.

item_4.reason: States 'medical databases systematically' were searched with dates, but does not name specific databases (e.g., MEDLINE, Embase).

item_5.reason: Risk of bias assessment methods stated: randomization, allocation concealment, blinding, incomplete outcome data, selective outcome reporting.

item_6.reason: Synthesis methods described: random-effects meta-analysis, trial sequential analysis (TSA), and multivariate meta-regression with preselected covariates.

item_7.reason: Reports 15 studies (n=18,474) with breakdown by study types: six RCTs, two cluster RCTs, five case-control, two cohort studies.

item_8.reason: Main results provided with effect size: OR=1.16 (95% CI: 0.97-1.39), p=0.11, I²=0.83, plus TSA findings.

item_9.reason: Abstract does not explicitly describe limitations of the evidence, study quality issues, or methodological constraints of the review.

item_10.reason: Interpretation provided: no improvement in ROSC with mechanical devices; potential benefit in specific circumstances; further studies may be futile.

item_11.reason: No registration information provided (e.g., PROSPERO registration number or registry name).

item_12.reason: No funding source or financial support information reported in the abstract.

paper_evaluations.title: Association between supportive interventions and healthcare utilization and outcomes in patients on long-term prescribed opioid therapy presenting to acute healthcare settings: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

1.reason: Title explicitly states 'a systematic review and meta-analysis'

2.reason: Abstract lacks registration number; PROSPERO registration only mentioned in methods section

3.reason: Background describes opioid epidemic, complications, and knowledge gaps justifying the review

4.reason: Objectives explicitly state PICOS elements: patients, interventions, outcomes, and acute care settings

5.reason: PROSPERO registration (CRD42018088962) and registration date (2018/02/19) clearly reported

6.reason: Detailed eligibility criteria provided with patient and study-related inclusion/exclusion criteria and rationale

7.reason: Multiple databases listed with date ranges; last search date April 11, 2019 specified

8.reason: Table 1 provides complete MEDLINE search strategy with all terms and limits

9.reason: Two reviewers independently screened titles/abstracts and full texts; duplicate review and disagreement resolution described

10.reason: Data extraction methods mentioned but not explicitly stated as done independently or in duplicate

11.reason: Variables extracted described and referenced in Supplementary Appendix 1

12.reason: Newcastle-Ottawa Scale for cohorts and Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool for RCTs specified

13.reason: Ratio of means with 95% CI specified as principal summary measure

14.reason: DerSimonian-Laird random effects method with inverse variance weighting and I² for heterogeneity described

15.reason: No assessment of publication bias or selective reporting across studies described

16.reason: Subgroup analysis separating RCTs from cohort studies described with rationale

17.reason: 5664 studies screened, 19 included reported; PRISMA diagram referenced (Fig. 1)

18.reason: Table 2 presents study characteristics including setting, subjects, interventions, outcomes, and follow-up

19.reason: Tables 3 and 4 present NOS scores for cohorts and Cochrane assessments for RCTs

20.reason: Individual study effect estimates not systematically presented; figures referenced but not shown in text

21.reason: Meta-analysis results presented with effect estimates, 95% CIs, and I² values

22.reason: No results of publication bias or selective reporting assessment presented

23.reason: Subgroup results for RCTs versus cohort studies presented separately

24.reason: Discussion summarizes main findings, strength of evidence, and relevance to healthcare providers and policy

25.reason: Dedicated strengths and limitations section discusses heterogeneity, inclusion rate, scope, and generalizability

26.reason: Conclusion provides interpretation of results and implications for future research and policy

27.reason: Alberta SPOR SUPPORT Unit funding and role (librarian, statistician, coordinator) clearly described

item_1.reason: Title explicitly states 'a systematic review and meta-analysis'

item_2.reason: Background provided but no explicit statement of main objective or research question

item_3.reason: Six explicit inclusion criteria listed including population, intervention, setting, and outcomes

item_4.reason: Information sources (databases) and search dates are not reported

item_5.reason: Methods for assessing risk of bias in included studies not described

item_6.reason: Synthesis methods described: qualitative analysis, data pooling, and meta-analysis using ratio of means

item_7.reason: Total included studies (19) reported but total number of participants not provided

item_8.reason: Main outcomes reported with effect sizes, confidence intervals, heterogeneity statistics (I²), and study numbers

item_9.reason: No limitations of the evidence or review discussed in abstract

item_10.reason: Interpretation provided: identifies 'supports for patients in pain' as intervention with most robust data

item_11.reason: No funding information provided in abstract

item_12.reason: No protocol registration information provided in abstract

paper_evaluations.title: Comparison of 3-factor versus 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate for emergent warfarin reversal: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

1.reason: Title explicitly identifies study as 'a systematic review and meta-analysis'

2.reason: Abstract has structured sections: Background, Methods, Results, Conclusion

3.reason: Rationale clearly described in Background regarding gap in PCC3 vs PCC4 comparisons

4.reason: Primary and secondary objectives explicitly stated: INR goal achievement, thromboembolic events, survival

5.reason: No protocol registration information or registration number provided

6.reason: Detailed inclusion and exclusion criteria listed in Study selection section

7.reason: Four databases specified (MEDLINE, Cochrane, EMBASE, Scopus) with dates (inception to August 20, 2020)

8.reason: Full search strategy provided in Additional file 1: Appendix A

9.reason: Two independent screeners, duplicate removal, conflict resolution by consensus described

10.reason: Four independent extractors using customized form after piloting described

11.reason: Comprehensive list of extracted variables provided including demographics, outcomes, doses

12.reason: Newcastle-Ottawa Scale described with scoring thresholds for bias risk

13.reason: Odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals specified as summary measures

14.reason: Random-effects models, heterogeneity measures (τ2, Q-test, I2) comprehensively described

15.reason: Funnel plots with rank correlation and regression tests for publication bias described

16.reason: Leave-one-out sensitivity analyses and GLMM models described

17.reason: 1583 identified, 68 full-text screened, 15 included with PRISMA flow diagram

18.reason: Tables 1-6 provide comprehensive study characteristics, demographics, dosing, timing

19.reason: Table 1 presents quality scores and risk of bias assessments for each study

20.reason: Forest plots (Figures 2, 4, 6) display individual study effect sizes and confidence intervals

21.reason: Pooled OR = 3.61 (95% CI: 1.97-6.60) with heterogeneity measures reported for all outcomes

22.reason: Funnel plots shown (Figures 3, 5, 7) with statistical test results for asymmetry

23.reason: Sensitivity analysis ranges (OR 3.14-4.34) and GLMM results provided

24.reason: Discussion summarizes PCC4 superior for INR reversal with similar safety profile

25.reason: Limitations discussed: observational design, no RCTs, heterogeneity, temporal effects, reporting variability

26.reason: Conclusion states PCC4 more effective with similar safety; future research directions provided

27.reason: Funding section explicitly states 'No funding was received for any portion of this manuscript'

item_1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as 'a systematic review and meta-analysis'.

item_2.reason: Primary and secondary objectives are explicitly stated, addressing effectiveness and safety outcomes.

item_3.reason: Eligibility criteria for study inclusion and exclusion are not specified in the abstract.

item_4.reason: Information sources (databases) and search dates are not reported in the abstract.

item_5.reason: Newcastle-Ottawa Scale is specified as the method used to assess study quality.

item_6.reason: Random-effects meta-analysis models for estimating odds ratios and heterogeneity are described.

item_7.reason: Number of included studies is reported (15), but total number of participants is not provided.

item_8.reason: Results for primary outcome (INR reversal) and secondary outcomes (TE, survival) are presented.

item_9.reason: Summary estimates (odds ratios) with 95% confidence intervals reported for all outcomes.

item_10.reason: Limitations of the evidence (e.g., study risk of bias, inconsistency) are not discussed.

item_11.reason: General interpretation provided: PCC4 more effective with similar safety profile compared to PCC3.

item_12.reason: Primary source of funding for the review is not specified in the abstract.

paper_evaluations.title: Cast versus removable orthosis for the management of stable type B ankle fractures: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

item_1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the study as 'a systematic review and meta-analysis.'

item_2.reason: Objective clearly stated: compare removable orthosis versus cast for safety and functional outcomes in stable type B ankle fractures.

item_3.reason: Abstract does not mention specific databases searched or search dates.

item_4.reason: Eligibility criteria specified: randomized clinical trials and observational studies were included.

item_5.reason: Participants described as adult patients with stable type B ankle fractures, total of 516 patients.

item_6.reason: Interventions clearly stated: removable orthosis compared to cast for nonoperative management.

item_7.reason: Quality assessment tool (methodological index for non-randomized studies) and outcome measures (OMAS) specified. Meta-analysis performed.

item_8.reason: Reports five included studies (two RCTs, three observational studies) with 516 total patients.

item_9.reason: Summary measures provided with confidence intervals: odds ratios and mean differences for complications and functional outcomes.

item_10.reason: Abstract does not mention any limitations of the review or quality of evidence.

item_11.reason: Conclusions provided: removable orthosis is safe alternative with lower complications and non-inferior functional outcomes.

item_12.reason: Abstract does not provide systematic review registration number or mention registration.

paper_evaluations.title: Clinical assessment and risk stratification for prehospital use of methoxyflurane versus standard analgesia in adult patients with trauma pain.

item_1.reason: Title does not explicitly identify the report as a systematic review or meta-analysis.

item_2.reason: Objectives clearly stated: assess clinical implications and risk stratification of low-dose methoxyflurane versus standard analgesics.

item_3.reason: Eligibility criteria specified: RCTs, adult patients, moderate-to-severe trauma pain, prehospital settings, comparison with standard analgesics/placebo.

item_4.reason: Information sources not specified. No databases named, no search dates provided.

item_5.reason: No methods for assessing risk of bias in individual studies are described.

item_6.reason: Principal summary measure stated: mean difference with 95% confidence interval for time to pain relief.

item_7.reason: Number of studies provided (4 RCTs) but total number of participants and important characteristics not reported.

item_8.reason: Key results presented with effect size and confidence interval: mean difference = -6.63, 95% CI = -7.37, -5.09.

item_9.reason: No discussion of limitations, study risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision of included evidence.

item_10.reason: General interpretation provided: low-dose methoxyflurane provides superior and faster pain relief compared to standard analgesics.

item_11.reason: No funding sources or support described in the abstract.

item_12.reason: No registration number or registry name provided for the systematic review.

paper_evaluations.title: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis Comparing the Safety and Efficacy of Spinal Anesthesia and Spinal Anesthesia Combined with Obturator Nerve Block in Transurethral Resection of Bladder Tumors.

1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis'

2.reason: Structured abstract provided with Background, Methods, Results, and Conclusion sections

3.reason: Rationale described including bladder cancer context, TURBT challenges, and previous meta-analysis limitations

4.reason: Objectives stated: 'to further clarify the effectiveness of ONB-assisted in TURBT' with PICOS elements

5.reason: No mention of protocol registration or registration number anywhere in the manuscript

6.reason: Eligibility criteria detailed in Section 2.1: study types, subjects, interventions, outcomes, languages, and dates

7.reason: Five databases specified (PubMed, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, CNKI, Wanfang) with dates January 2000-December 2021

8.reason: Search strategy provided: ('obturator nerve block'|'ONB') AND ('transurethral resection of bladder tumors'|TURBT) with filters

9.reason: Selection process described: two researchers independently screened, used EndNote X9, removed duplicates, third person for disputes

10.reason: Data extraction described in Section 2.4: two researchers extracted literature characteristics, subject information, interventions, outcomes

11.reason: Data items listed: bladder perforation, obturator reflex, hospital stay, tumor recurrence, plus study characteristics

12.reason: Risk of bias assessed using Cochrane Handbook with 6 aspects and Jadad scale described

13.reason: Summary measures stated: risk ratio (RR) for dichotomous outcomes, mean difference (MD) for continuous outcomes

14.reason: Synthesis methods described: R 4.1.2, fixed/random effects models based on I²>50%, forest plots, heterogeneity assessment

15.reason: Publication bias assessment specified: funnel plot used to represent publication bias (Section 2.6)

16.reason: Additional analyses described: subgroup analysis by surgical approach, sensitivity analysis by sequential exclusion

17.reason: Study selection numbers provided: 444 initially retrieved, 8 included, 635 patients; flow diagram in Figure 1

18.reason: Table 1 presents study characteristics for each included study: author, year, age, methods, population, outcomes

19.reason: Table 2 presents risk of bias for each study using Cochrane criteria; Section 3.3 discusses findings

20.reason: Forest plots (Figures 2-5) show individual study data, effect estimates, and confidence intervals for all outcomes

21.reason: Meta-analysis results presented with effect sizes, 95% CIs, I² values, and p-values for all outcomes

22.reason: Section 3.4.6 reports publication bias analysis with funnel plot (Figure 7), noting small publication bias

23.reason: Section 3.4.5 presents sensitivity analysis (Figure 6) and subgroup analyses by surgical approach in results

24.reason: Discussion summarizes main findings about reduced obturator reflex, bladder perforation, hospital stay, and tumor recurrence

25.reason: Limitations explicitly discussed: small study number, potential bias from inadequate blinding/concealment, limited subgroup comparisons

26.reason: Conclusion interprets results and states need for more high-quality, multicenter, large-sample RCTs

27.reason: No funding section or statement describing funding sources for the systematic review

item_1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as both a systematic review and meta-analysis.

item_2.reason: Objective clearly stated: to evaluate co-application of ONB and spinal anesthesia in TURBT.

item_3.reason: Specifies RCTs, specific intervention (ONB combined with SA), procedure (TURBT), and date range (2000-2021).

item_4.reason: Five databases named (PubMed, EMBASE, Cochrane, CNKI, Wanfang) with search dates January 2000-December 2021.

item_5.reason: Jadad scale explicitly mentioned as the method for assessing literature quality and risk of bias.

item_6.reason: Meta-analysis using R language toolkit is specified as the synthesis method.

item_7.reason: Reports 8 included studies and 635 total patients, with initial retrieval of 444 articles.

item_8.reason: Provides effect sizes (RR/MD), 95% confidence intervals, Z-scores, and P-values for all four main outcomes.

item_9.reason: No limitations, strengths, or weaknesses of the review are discussed in the abstract.

item_10.reason: Conclusion clearly interprets results showing multiple benefits of SA combined with ONB in TURBT.

item_11.reason: No funding source or financial support information is mentioned in the abstract.

item_12.reason: No registration number or registry name for the systematic review is provided.

paper_evaluations.title: Efficacy of ketorolac in the treatment of acute migraine attack: A systematic review and meta-analysis.

item_1.reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A systematic review and meta-analysis.'

item_2.reason: Objective clearly stated: 'evaluate the efficacy of parenteral ketorolac in treating acute migraine headache.'

item_3.reason: Eligibility criteria specified: randomized controlled trials comparing ketorolac to other medications in migraine patients.

item_4.reason: Information sources listed (CENTRAL, Medline, Google Scholar) with search date (up to January 2021).

item_5.reason: No mention of methods used to assess risk of bias in included studies.

item_6.reason: Synthesis methods indicated: meta-analysis with standard mean difference (SMD) and risk ratio (RR) reported.

item_7.reason: Total studies (13 trials), participants (944), and seven comparison groups explicitly stated.

item_8.reason: Results presented for multiple main outcomes with effect estimates (SMD, RR) and p-values.

item_9.reason: Limitation acknowledged: 'lack of evidence due to inadequate number of trials available.'

item_10.reason: General interpretation provided regarding ketorolac's similar or superior efficacy compared to various comparators.

item_11.reason: No mention of funding sources for the review.

item_12.reason: No protocol registration number or registry name provided.

Objective clearly stated: compare postoperative recovery after LA versus OA to determine optimal treatment plan for appendectomy.

Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis and Trial Sequential Analysis.'
Objective clearly stated: comparing mechanical compression devices versus manual compression for achieving ROSC after OHCA.
Eligibility criteria specified: RCTs and observational studies, 2000-2020, English language, adult patients, non-trauma OHCA, ROSC outcomes.
States 'medical databases systematically' were searched with dates, but does not name specific databases (e.g., MEDLINE, Embase).
Risk of bias assessment methods stated: randomization, allocation concealment, blinding, incomplete outcome data, selective outcome reporting.
Synthesis methods described: random-effects meta-analysis, trial sequential analysis (TSA), and multivariate meta-regression with preselected covariates.
Reports 15 studies (n=18,474) with breakdown by study types: six RCTs, two cluster RCTs, five case-control, two cohort studies.
Main results provided with effect size: OR=1.16 (95% CI: 0.97-1.39), p=0.11, I²=0.83, plus TSA findings.
Abstract does not explicitly describe limitations of the evidence, study quality issues, or methodological constraints of the review.
Interpretation provided: no improvement in ROSC with mechanical devices; potential benefit in specific circumstances; further studies may be futile.
No registration information provided (e.g., PROSPERO registration number or registry name).
No funding source or financial support information reported in the abstract.

Detailed inclusion and exclusion criteria listed in Study selection section

comparison_details.title: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis Comparing the Safety and Efficacy of Spinal Anesthesia and Spinal Anesthesia Combined with Obturator Nerve Block in Transurethral Resection of Bladder Tumors.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis'
Rationale described including bladder cancer context, TURBT challenges, and previous meta-analysis limitations
Objectives stated: 'to further clarify the effectiveness of ONB-assisted in TURBT' with PICOS elements
No mention of protocol registration or registration number anywhere in the manuscript
Eligibility criteria detailed in Section 2.1: study types, subjects, interventions, outcomes, languages, and dates
Five databases specified (PubMed, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, CNKI, Wanfang) with dates January 2000-December 2021
Search strategy provided: ('obturator nerve block'|'ONB') AND ('transurethral resection of bladder tumors'|TURBT) with filters
Selection process described: two researchers independently screened, used EndNote X9, removed duplicates, third person for disputes
Data extraction described in Section 2.4: two researchers extracted literature characteristics, subject information, interventions, outcomes
Data items listed: bladder perforation, obturator reflex, hospital stay, tumor recurrence, plus study characteristics
Risk of bias assessed using Cochrane Handbook with 6 aspects and Jadad scale described
Summary measures stated: risk ratio (RR) for dichotomous outcomes, mean difference (MD) for continuous outcomes
Synthesis methods described: R 4.1.2, fixed/random effects models based on I²>50%, forest plots, heterogeneity assessment
Publication bias assessment specified: funnel plot used to represent publication bias (Section 2.6)
Additional analyses described: subgroup analysis by surgical approach, sensitivity analysis by sequential exclusion
Study selection numbers provided: 444 initially retrieved, 8 included, 635 patients; flow diagram in Figure 1
Table 1 presents study characteristics for each included study: author, year, age, methods, population, outcomes
Table 2 presents risk of bias for each study using Cochrane criteria; Section 3.3 discusses findings
Forest plots (Figures 2-5) show individual study data, effect estimates, and confidence intervals for all outcomes
Meta-analysis results presented with effect sizes, 95% CIs, I² values, and p-values for all outcomes
Section 3.4.6 reports publication bias analysis with funnel plot (Figure 7), noting small publication bias
Section 3.4.5 presents sensitivity analysis (Figure 6) and subgroup analyses by surgical approach in results
Discussion summarizes main findings about reduced obturator reflex, bladder perforation, hospital stay, and tumor recurrence
Limitations explicitly discussed: small study number, potential bias from inadequate blinding/concealment, limited subgroup comparisons
Conclusion interprets results and states need for more high-quality, multicenter, large-sample RCTs
No funding section or statement describing funding sources for the systematic review

Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A systematic review and meta-analysis.'
Objective clearly stated: 'evaluate the efficacy of parenteral ketorolac in treating acute migraine headache.'
Eligibility criteria specified: randomized controlled trials comparing ketorolac to other medications in migraine patients.
Information sources listed (CENTRAL, Medline, Google Scholar) with search date (up to January 2021).
No mention of methods used to assess risk of bias in included studies.
Synthesis methods indicated: meta-analysis with standard mean difference (SMD) and risk ratio (RR) reported.
Total studies (13 trials), participants (944), and seven comparison groups explicitly stated.
Results presented for multiple main outcomes with effect estimates (SMD, RR) and p-values.
Limitation acknowledged: 'lack of evidence due to inadequate number of trials available.'
General interpretation provided regarding ketorolac's similar or superior efficacy compared to various comparators.
No mention of funding sources for the review.
No protocol registration number or registry name provided.
